title: Claiming Society for God: Religious Movements and Social Welfare By Nancy J. Davis and Robert V. Robinson Indiana University Press. 2012. 234 pages. $25 paper
abstract: Political sociology and social movement studies focus mostly on mobilizations that either seek change within existing systems or aim to overthrow reigning institutions. The sociology of religion remains centered on determinants of worship frequency and other debates that interest the broader social sciences only marginally. Davis and Robertson's book directs attention to sociologically much more challenging phenomena. Claiming Society for God wants us to pay attention to the world-building power of religion in its capacity to reconstitute states, economies, and societies. The religious movements they cover engage in a different kind of politics, the success of which we are poorly equipped to measure. The book is based on a study of four religious movements: the Salvation Army USA, the Egyptian Muslim Brotherhood, the Israeli Shas, and the Italian Comunione e Liberazione. Davis and Robertson make crucial theoretical and empirical contributions throughout their discussion of these world-transforming movements. On the theoretical front, the authors develop a quasi-Gramscian framework to analyze how orthodox movements surmount internal and external impediments, but their analyses can inform the study of non-orthodox (or even non-religious) movements as well. Orthodox movements grow despite three ingrained characteristics that have been defined as deadly in social movement scholarship: broad agendas, ideological rigidity, and reluctance to compromise.
